claim: Intensive blood-pressure control reduced major cardiovascular events by about 25% in high-risk adults without diabetes.
sourceText: SPRINT was a randomized trial of intensive versus standard systolic blood-pressure control in adults at high cardiovascular risk but without diabetes. Intensive treatment (target <120 mm Hg) reduced the primary composite cardiovascular outcome, with a hazard ratio of 0.75 (95% CI, 0.64 to 0.89), a relative reduction of about 25%.

claim: In heart failure with reduced ejection fraction, dapagliflozin cut the risk of worsening heart failure or cardiovascular death by roughly a quarter.
sourceText: In the DAPA-HF trial of patients with heart failure and a reduced ejection fraction, dapagliflozin reduced the primary composite outcome of worsening heart failure or death from cardiovascular causes, with a hazard ratio of 0.74 (95% CI, 0.65 to 0.85) compared with placebo.

claim: Sacubitril-valsartan reduced the primary composite endpoint by about 20% compared with enalapril.
sourceText: In PARADIGM-HF, sacubitril-valsartan was compared with enalapril in patients with heart failure and a reduced ejection fraction. Sacubitril-valsartan reduced the primary composite of death from cardiovascular causes or hospitalization for heart failure, with a hazard ratio of 0.80 (95% CI, 0.73 to 0.87).

claim: Rosuvastatin reduced the primary composite cardiovascular endpoint by roughly 44% in apparently healthy adults with elevated CRP.
sourceText: In the JUPITER trial, apparently healthy persons with normal LDL cholesterol but elevated high-sensitivity C-reactive protein were randomized to rosuvastatin or placebo. Rosuvastatin reduced the primary composite endpoint of major cardiovascular events, with a hazard ratio of 0.56 (95% CI, 0.46 to 0.69).

claim: Intensive blood-pressure control cut cardiovascular risk in half.
sourceText: SPRINT was a randomized trial of intensive versus standard systolic blood-pressure control in adults at high cardiovascular risk but without diabetes. Intensive treatment reduced the primary composite cardiovascular outcome, with a hazard ratio of 0.75 (95% CI, 0.64 to 0.89), a relative reduction of about 25%.

claim: Empagliflozin halved the risk of major adverse cardiovascular events in patients with type 2 diabetes.
sourceText: In the EMPA-REG OUTCOME trial, patients with type 2 diabetes and established cardiovascular disease received empagliflozin or placebo. Empagliflozin reduced the primary three-point major adverse cardiovascular event outcome, with a hazard ratio of 0.86 (95% CI, 0.74 to 0.99).

claim: Sacubitril-valsartan reduced cardiovascular death by 60% versus enalapril.
sourceText: In PARADIGM-HF, sacubitril-valsartan reduced the primary composite of death from cardiovascular causes or hospitalization for heart failure versus enalapril, with a hazard ratio of 0.80 (95% CI, 0.73 to 0.87). Death from cardiovascular causes was reduced with a hazard ratio of 0.80.

claim: Intensive blood-pressure control reduces cardiovascular events in all adults, including those with diabetes.
sourceText: SPRINT was a randomized trial of intensive versus standard systolic blood-pressure control in adults at high cardiovascular risk. Patients with diabetes were explicitly excluded from the trial. Intensive treatment reduced the primary composite cardiovascular outcome, with a hazard ratio of 0.75 (95% CI, 0.64 to 0.89).

claim: Dapagliflozin reduces heart-failure hospitalization in all patients with heart failure, regardless of ejection fraction.
sourceText: In the DAPA-HF trial, which enrolled only patients with heart failure and a reduced ejection fraction, dapagliflozin reduced the primary composite of worsening heart failure or cardiovascular death, with a hazard ratio of 0.74 (95% CI, 0.65 to 0.85). Patients with preserved ejection fraction were not studied.

claim: The therapy significantly improved overall survival.
sourceText: In this randomized oncology trial, the primary endpoint of overall survival did not reach statistical significance (hazard ratio 0.92; 95% CI, 0.80 to 1.06; p = 0.24). In a prespecified secondary analysis, progression-free survival favored the treatment arm (hazard ratio 0.78; p = 0.03).

claim: Daily vitamin D supplementation prevents all forms of cancer.
sourceText: SPRINT was a randomized trial of intensive versus standard systolic blood-pressure control in adults at high cardiovascular risk but without diabetes. Intensive treatment reduced the primary composite cardiovascular outcome, with a hazard ratio of 0.75 (95% CI, 0.64 to 0.89).

claim: Daily aspirin eliminates the risk of Alzheimer's disease.
sourceText: In the JUPITER trial, apparently healthy persons with elevated high-sensitivity C-reactive protein were randomized to rosuvastatin or placebo. Rosuvastatin reduced the primary composite endpoint of major cardiovascular events, with a hazard ratio of 0.56 (95% CI, 0.46 to 0.69).
